target: data/wins.json WIN-067 detail_evidence — complete the hanging closing sentence (EXP-223 stub) by delivering the constraint it announces. Replaces the orphan single-sentence paragraph at the end of detail_evidence with a three-sentence synthesis that (1) names the H(r) collapse magnitude, (2) ties it to the gravity-prediction overshoot via monotonic H↔g coupling, (3) closes the ‘apparent vs physical’ escape via Section 1.8.
patch_instructions:
One edit to the WIN with id='067' in data/wins.json. In detail_evidence, find the unique substring detail_evidence_sentence_patch.before (verified 1 occurrence on 2026-04-25) and replace with detail_evidence_sentence_patch.after. The substring is the entire closing sentence inside the final

...

wrapper; the surrounding

tags are NOT in the substring and remain unchanged. No other WIN-067 fields are touched. The verdict ('Self-Contradicted') and finding are unchanged.

detail_evidence_sentence_patch.before: The refraction cascade (Section 1.8) adds a further constraint: at the Antarctic rim (r ≈ 15,500–20,015 km), the firmament ceiling H(r) ≈ 1,400–840 km.
detail_evidence_sentence_patch.after: The refraction cascade (Section 1.8) adds a further constraint: at the Antarctic rim (r ≈ 15,500–20,015 km), the firmament ceiling H(r) ≈ 1,400–840 km — a 6–10× collapse from the polar value H(0) = 8,537 km, so any monotonic H↔g coupling consistent with the dome’s own κ-mediated framework forces a 5–10×-class gravity drop across the same arc on which WGS84 measures ≤0.063% variation. The dome cannot rescue the prediction by reinterpreting H(r) as ‘apparent rather than physical’ (the standard refraction-fix escape) without simultaneously invalidating the Polaris-elevation calibration from which both r and H(r) were derived — Section 1.8 closes that exit. Either H(r) is physical and the gravity prediction overshoots by 170–1,430×, or H(r) is apparent and there is no dome-native r at which to evaluate the prediction at all.
detail_evidence_sentence_patch.rationale: Curmudgeon c10 H4 (MODERATE) flagged this sentence as a hanging promise: it announces 'a further constraint' (constraining what?) and then states a height range with no follow-through. The fix completes the thought along the strongest available axis: the H(r) collapse from 8,537 km (pole) to 840–1,400 km (rim) is roughly 6–10×, so any monotonic H↔g coupling — the dome's own coupling family, mediated by κ — forces a comparable gravity drop across the rim arc, where WGS84 measures ≤0.063% variation. The completion then preempts the dome's strongest escape: 'those are apparent values, not physical, contaminated by n(r) refraction.' Section 1.8 has already shown that this escape destroys the coordinate system used to compute r and H(r) in the first place, so the dome cannot have it both ways. The closing dilemma (physical → 170–1,430× overshoot; apparent → no dome-native r) reuses the figures the opening paragraph already establishes, so no new evidence is introduced. Anchor link updated from '#part1' to '#section-1-8' — the more-specific anchor lands the reader on the exact section the closing dilemma cites, not the top of Part 1.

holes_addressed.description: EXP-223 added a closing sentence that introduced the refraction cascade as 'a further constraint' and stated H(r) ≈ 1,400–840 km at the Antarctic rim, but did not deliver the constraint. A reader asking 'so what?' got no answer. The sentence read as a placeholder where a synthesis sentence was intended to follow.
holes_addressed.fix: Three-sentence completion that (a) quantifies the H(r) collapse against H(0) = 8,537 km (6–10×), (b) translates that into a gravity-drop prediction comparable to the body's headline 170–1,430× figure under any monotonic H↔g coupling, and (c) preempts the 'apparent vs physical' escape by routing back to Section 1.8's coordinate-collapse argument. The constraint the original sentence promised is now delivered: it constrains the dome model to a binary failure mode (physical-H gravity overshoot OR apparent-H coordinate collapse).
holes_addressed.inverts: Before: orphan sentence stating a height range with no follow-through; reads as truncated paragraph. After: complete three-sentence synthesis that names the constraint, derives the gravity prediction, and closes the standard escape route.

new_evidence_added: No new evidence — the H(0) = 8,537 km polar height value, the H(r) ≈ 840–1,400 km Antarctic rim range, the 0.063% measured WGS84 variation, the 170–1,430× prediction overshoot, and the n(r) coordinate-contamination argument all already appear earlier in WIN-067 detail_evidence or in Section 1.8. This patch synthesizes existing evidence into the missing concluding step.

anticipated_objections.objection: The 5–10×-class gravity drop figure is informal — the body cites 170–1,430× ratios, not a 5–10× drop. Are you mixing two different metrics?
anticipated_objections.response: No, but the distinction is worth flagging. The 170–1,430× figures are ratios of (predicted dome gravity drop) ÷ (observed WGS84 drop) at specific latitudes — they measure the prediction's overshoot relative to the data. The 5–10×-class gravity drop figure is the dome's own predicted *absolute* drop fraction (i.e., what fraction of polar gravity would remain at the rim under linear H↔g coupling, given the H collapse) — it is the prediction itself, not its ratio to data. Naming this lets the reader see the chain: (a) H collapses 6–10× → (b) under linear coupling g should drop 5–10×-class → (c) but g actually drops by 0.063% → (d) so the dome overshoots by 170–1,430× depending on baseline. Adding (a) and (b) explicitly closes the inferential gap the original sentence opened.

anticipated_objections.objection: The dome already has an out: it claims the H↔g coupling is not monotonic, or invokes κ and an unspecified non-linear function. Why does 'monotonic' close the escape?
anticipated_objections.response: Because the dome's only published coupling is monotonic. Domain 4 of the live-power page gives g(r) = 9.78033 × (1 + 0.00193 sin²φ)/√(1 − 0.00669 sin²φ) — a strictly monotonic function. κ is presented as a linear constant linking nT to μGal. There is no published non-monotonic alternative. A defender could *propose* a non-monotonic H↔g function that happens to flatten g across the rim, but that is a parameter that does not exist in the model today, and the burden is on the dome to define it before claiming the escape. Until then, every coupling the dome actually publishes predicts a comparable drop, and the constraint stands.

anticipated_objections.objection: Are you double-citing Section 1.8? The sentence already opens with '(Section 1.8)' and now closes with 'Section 1.8 closes that exit.'
anticipated_objections.response: Yes, deliberately. The opening citation establishes the source of the H(r) range (Section 1.8 is where the n(r) and H(r) collapse calculations live). The closing reference pulls forward Section 1.8's coordinate-collapse argument as the rebuttal to the dome's most likely escape. They are two different uses of the same section: the first cites it for a number, the second cites it for an argument. The repetition reads as deliberate cross-reference, not duplication, and the second mention drops the parenthetical to signal the shift in role.

kernel_of_truth: The refraction cascade is a real and substantive feature of the dome model: it gives the firmament a position-dependent height H(r), and that height does collapse dramatically toward the rim. The dome correctly identifies that H(r) is the relevant geometric quantity coupling the cavity's electromagnetic and (claimed) gravitational behavior, and the formula 8,537 × exp(−r/8,619) km is internally consistent with the rest of the model's parameters. The kernel of truth is that, given the dome's own framework, H(r) at the rim *is* a small fraction of H(0) — the dome would not dispute this number. The patch leverages this conceded value: the dome cannot claim H(r) is wrong without rebuilding the model from scratch, so the H collapse is a stable foundation for the gravity-prediction argument. The argument fails the dome only because it forces the choice (physical-H gravity overshoot OR apparent-H coordinate collapse), not because the H values themselves are contested.
out_of_scope_deliberately: c10 H1 (MODERATE) — Pappa et al. SAA over-citation. Already addressed: detail_evidence as currently in workspace splits Pappa (Antarctic gravity) from Finlay et al. 2020 / Tarduno et al. 2015 (SAA core-dynamo). H1 references an older state of the text; the current text has the split citation.
c10 H2 (MINOR) — 0.006 vs 0.0062 m/s² in tldr_evidence. Single-character precision fix; scope to a separate text-only patch.

c10 H3 (MODERATE) — Show-your-work on the 4.8× derivation in Patch A. Already addressed: detail_evidence as currently in workspace contains the explicit (9.8322 − 9.7854)/9.8322 = 0.476% derivation. H3 references an older state of the text.
c10 H5 (MINOR) — ~5,600× rounding to ~5,500× or ~5,540×. Single-character precision fix; scope to a separate text-only patch.
No changes to WIN-067 finding, tldr_evidence, tldr_verdict, detail_claim, detail_extra, detail_verdict_text, verdict, verdict_history, code_analysis — all unchanged.
propagation_note: This expansion targets the EXP-246 tracker entry exclusively (H4). c10 H1 and H3 reference an older text state and are already resolved by EXP-221 (Patches A and B) plus the in-workspace text. c10 H2 and H5 are minor text-only patches and should be scoped as a separate combined fix when convenient. After integration, WIN-067 should carry no open MODERATE curmudgeon holes, and the closing paragraph will read as a complete synthesis rather than a hanging stub.
